title: Matted Fur on a Cat's Spine and Hips: Causes and Care Tips
meta_desc: Learn why cats get mats on their back and hips, when it's a concern, and how photos help your vet provide the best triage advice for your pet.
content_html:
What is Matted Fur?

Matted fur occurs when your cat's hair becomes tangled, knotted, and tightly wound against the skin. Along the spine and hips, this often happens because these areas are harder for a cat to reach during their self-grooming routine. While it might look like a simple cosmetic issue, mats can trap moisture and bacteria against the skin, leading to irritation or infection.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for matted fur is Low. In most cases, this is a chronic issue rather than a sudden medical crisis. However, while it is not an emergency, it does require timely attention. Mats can be painful as they pull on the skin and may hide underlying skin lesions. If your cat is also showing signs of pain, lethargy, or if the skin looks raw, you should consult your veterinarian within a few days.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Providing a high-quality photo of the mats and the surrounding skin is incredibly beneficial for a triage assessment. A photo allows a professional to see the proximity of the mat to the skin, the presence of any redness or inflammation, and the overall condition of the coat. Because mats on the spine often point toward mobility issues like arthritis or weight problems, seeing the distribution of the mats helps determine if a physical exam is needed to check your cat's flexibility and joint health.

faqs.question: Can I use scissors to cut out my cat's mats?
faqs.answer: No, it is highly recommended to avoid using scissors. Cat skin is paper-thin and very elastic, making it extremely easy to accidentally cut the skin while trying to remove a mat. Professional grooming clippers are much safer.

faqs.question: Why is my cat suddenly stopping grooming their back?
faqs.answer: Sudden lack of grooming along the spine and hips is often a sign of discomfort, such as arthritis, dental pain, or obesity, which prevents the cat from reaching those specific areas.

faqs.question: Will matted fur go away on its own?
faqs.answer: No, mats will typically get tighter and larger over time if not physically removed. They do not resolve through self-grooming and often require professional intervention.
